claim: EVA vowel patterns encode section/domain content independent of the consonant skeleton. Specifically: pattern '_.eo' predicts pharmaceutical section modality at 100% across 8 unrelated skeletons; pattern '_.o.e' predicts biological at 100% across 3 skeletons; pattern '_.e' predicts biological at 58% across 12 skeletons (vs 30.5% random baseline). A naive-Bayes classifier using vowel-pattern features alone achieves 27.2% 5-fold CV accuracy vs 21.1% majority baseline (+6.1pp).
evidence_for: _.eo pattern: 8/8 skeletons modal to pharmaceutical (100% agreement, 3.3x over baseline)
Skeleton kdy internal consistency: chedy/okedy/okeedy (e-vowels) all biological at 2.0-2.2x enrichment, chody (o-vowel) pharmaceutical/herbal at 2.0x
Cross-skeleton generalisation: vowel pattern carries meaning not derivable from consonant frame
Classifier accuracy 27.2% vs 21.1% majority = predictive info above chance
evidence_against: Sample sizes are small for 100%-agreement patterns (n=3-8 skeletons)
Classifier gain of +6.1pp is modest; vowel patterns explain only a fraction of section variance
Vowel-section coupling could reflect scribal production phases rather than a designed encoding
test: Held-out prediction: pick 2 pharmaceutical folios Brain-V has never scored, predict section content from vowel patterns of their tokens, measure accuracy vs baseline.
test_threshold: >=35%: strong support. 25-35%: consistent with training signal. <20%: training-set artefact.

tests_run.details: 55 skeleton groups significant at p<0.01; 100% agreement on _.eo (n=8) and _.o.e (n=3); classifier gain +6.1pp over majority baseline

tests_remaining: Consonant+vowel combined classifier (does skeleton add signal?)
Currier A vs B consistency of vowel-section rule
reasoning: First working language-independent decode signal. Does not depend on dictionary matching. Challenges the coverage-game paradigm by demonstrating that EVA vowels carry exploitable structure without reference to any natural language lexicon. Strategic pivot anchor.
